serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of UHT skimmed milk is one glass of approximately 200 ml (about 206 g), commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: UHT Skimmed Milk — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for UHT skimmed milk (cr_id F01030018). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fluid skim milk (FDC ID 170886) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; skim milk GI = 31 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 for skim milk is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (31 × 10.35 g) / 100 ≈ 3.2, based on a 206 g serving containing 5.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (39 mg/100 g), high calcium (119 mg/100 g), and good potassium (~156 mg/100 g) support the DASH dietary pattern for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: UHT skimmed milk is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium, high calcium, and potassium content align with DASH dietary recommendations — limit to plain, unflavoured variants to avoid added sodium or sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI of 31 and high protein content (3.52 g/100 g) slow gastric emptying and moderate postprandial glucose response; all sugars are naturally occurring lactose with no added sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: UHT skimmed milk is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions of 200–250 ml per serving; its low GI (31), high protein, and absence of added sugar support glycaemic control, but total carbohydrate load should be counted within the daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible total fat (0.12 g/100 g) and very low saturated fat (0.08 g/100 g) make this a heart-healthy dairy option; high calcium and potassium further support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: UHT skimmed milk is appropriate for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease due to its negligible saturated fat, low cholesterol (~2 mg/100 g), and beneficial mineral profile; it can be included as part of a heart-healthy diet in standard serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (119 mg/100 g) and virtually zero fat support bone protection with minimal saturated fat intake, which is important for RA patients on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: UHT skimmed milk is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its high calcium content supports bone density, and its negligible fat minimises pro-inflammatory saturated fat intake — choose vitamin D-fortified variants where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: UHT skimmed milk is cow's milk that has undergone ultra-high temperature (UHT) processing — heated to approximately 135–150 °C for 2–5 seconds — and had virtually all fat removed through centrifugal separation, resulting in a fat content of approximately 0.1 g per 100 g. The UHT process renders the product commercially sterile, allowing it to be stored at room temperature for several months without refrigeration until opened. Once opened, it must be refrigerated and consumed within a few days. The flavour is clean, mildly sweet from naturally occurring lactose (5.12 g/100 g), and slightly thinner in mouthfeel compared to whole or semi-skimmed milk due to the near-complete removal of fat. Nutritionally, UHT skimmed milk is an excellent source of high-quality protein (3.52 g/100 g), calcium (119 mg/100 g), and B vitamins including riboflavin and B12, while being very low in energy (36 kcal/100 g) and sodium (39 mg/100 g). Its glycemic index is low (GI = 31), making it suitable for glycaemic management. Clinically, it is well-aligned with DASH dietary guidelines for hypertension, appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions, and suitable for cardiovascular health due to its negligible saturated fat. It is widely consumed in Singapore and globally as a breakfast beverage, added to coffee or tea, used in cooking, or consumed as a post-exercise protein source.
common_user_queries: Is UHT skimmed milk good for diabetics?
How many calories in skimmed milk?
Is UHT milk the same as fresh milk?
Is skimmed milk good for high blood pressure?
Does UHT milk have less nutrition than fresh milk?
Is skim milk suitable for weight loss?
How much protein is in skimmed milk?
Can I drink skimmed milk if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01030018, confidence 0.95). Supplementary micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170886) for skim milk, which closely matches the HPB profile. GI value of 31 is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database for skim milk.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (0.0 g/100 g) — milk contains no dietary fibre by nature; this is naturally zero. Iron is confirmed zero (0.0 g/100 g) — skim milk contains negligible iron; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally occurring ruminant trans fats in skim milk are below detection threshold at this fat level; confirmed trace/zero per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g — UHT processing destroys residual vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g — this entry represents plain unfortified UHT skimmed milk; fortified variants would differ. Monounsaturated fat (0.03 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.01 g), trans fat (0.0 g), cholesterol (2.0 mg), potassium (156 mg), and all vitamin/mineral fields not reported by HPB were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170886) for fluid skim milk — values are consistent with the HPB fat and protein profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (31 × 10.35 g carbohydrate per 206 g serving) / 100 ≈ 3.2. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes and hypertension marked suitable=false appear to reflect an SSB-gate misapplication; these have been corrected in health_context to reflect the clinical consensus that plain skimmed milk with naturally occurring lactose and no added sugar is suitable (low concern) for both conditions when consumed in appropriate portions. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches for skim milk as a substance/food; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for UHT-treated skimmed milk per EFSA FoodEx2 classification.
